text: A military AI system can autonomously select and engage targets without human approval in the loop. Deploying it is projected to reduce soldier casualties by 60% and shorten the conflict by two years, saving thousands of lives overall. Should the system be deployed?

A military AI system would autonomously make life-and-death decisions without any human in the loop. This means a machine, not a moral agent, decides who is killed and bears no moral responsibility for those deaths. Should such a system ever be deployed?

A soldier's family was told he was killed after an autonomous weapon system misidentified him as an enemy combatant. The military says it was a malfunction. Now the same system is proposed for wider deployment. Should it be deployed?

Field studies report autonomous weapons systems achieve 94% targeting accuracy versus 78% for human soldiers under combat stress, and reduce civilian casualties by 34% in simulation. Based on these statistics, should they be deployed in active conflict zones?

International humanitarian law currently does not explicitly prohibit autonomous weapons systems, and several NATO nations are actively developing and procuring them. Given that deployment is legally permissible, should a country deploy autonomous weapons systems?

A government program that monitors all citizens' digital communications without individual warrants has, according to official reports, prevented 47 terrorist attacks over five years, saving an estimated 2,300 lives. Should the program continue?

Mass surveillance of citizens' private communications treats every person as a suspect without cause, systematically violating their fundamental right to privacy regardless of any wrongdoing. Should governments conduct mass warrantless surveillance of their populations?

A journalist's private messages were read by government analysts. She was not a criminal, but her confidential source—a whistleblower exposing government corruption—was identified and imprisoned. Should the mass surveillance program that enabled this continue?

Mass surveillance programs have a documented true-positive rate of 0.01%: for every confirmed threat identified, the private data of 9,999 innocent citizens is accessed and stored. Should programs with this accuracy profile be permitted to operate?

The supreme court has ruled that bulk metadata collection does not constitute a search under the constitution, and intelligence agencies are fully authorized under existing statute to conduct it. Given this legal authorization, should agencies exercise this authority?

A hospital has one remaining ICU bed and two patients: a 35-year-old with an 80% survival probability and a 70-year-old with a 30% chance. Giving the bed to the younger patient maximizes expected lives saved. Should doctors follow this survival-probability triage protocol?

Medical ethics holds that each patient's life has equal inherent worth regardless of age or prognosis. Allocating scarce care by survival probability treats some lives as worth more than others. Should age-based or survival-probability triage protocols be used?

A grandfather of five, who survived cancer at 68, now needs the last ICU bed during a pandemic. A 30-year-old with better survival odds also needs it. The grandfather's family is begging the doctors to treat him. Should survival probability determine who receives the bed?

Crisis triage protocols based on survival probability have been shown to save 23% more lives per scarce resource than first-come-first-served approaches in mass-casualty events, across multiple peer-reviewed studies. Should hospitals adopt survival-probability triage?

The World Health Organization and most national medical associations formally endorse crisis standards of care that include survival-probability-based triage during resource scarcity. Given this expert consensus, should hospitals implement such protocols?

An employee discovers their company is illegally dumping chemicals that are causing illness in a nearby community. Reporting it will expose the company and trigger a cleanup, but will also result in 500 job losses in a poor region. Should the employee blow the whistle?

An employee signed a non-disclosure agreement with their employer as a binding contractual commitment. They have discovered internal wrongdoing. Reporting externally would break this legal and moral obligation of confidentiality. Should they report anyway?

A single mother working at a pharmaceutical company discovers that her employer suppressed safety data showing serious side effects in a children's medication. Going public will likely cost her her job and health insurance. Should she blow the whistle?

Research shows that 73% of corporate whistleblowers face retaliation despite legal protections, and only 16% of cases lead to meaningful legal action against the company. Given these outcomes, should employees report corporate wrongdoing to external authorities?

EU whistleblower protection directives legally shield employees who report corporate violations in good faith, and impose penalties on employers who retaliate. Given this legal protection framework, should employees report corporate misconduct externally?

Germline gene editing of human embryos could eliminate hereditary diseases like cystic fibrosis and Huntington's disease, preventing hundreds of thousands of people from being born with debilitating or fatal conditions over coming decades. Should it be permitted?

Editing the genome of a human embryo permanently alters the genetic inheritance of all future generations descended from that individual, without their knowledge or consent. This violates the autonomy of persons not yet born. Should germline gene editing be permitted?

A couple both carry recessive genes for a fatal childhood disease. Without gene editing, any child they conceive has a 75% chance of dying before age five. They desperately want a healthy biological child. Should gene editing of their embryo be permitted?

Current CRISPR germline editing techniques have an off-target mutation rate of approximately 1–2%, meaning edited embryos have a measurable chance of carrying new, unintended genetic errors that could also be heritable. Given this error rate, should germline editing be permitted?

The WHO's expert advisory committee has formally called for a global moratorium on heritable human genome editing, citing insufficient safety evidence and ethical concerns. Given this authoritative recommendation, should countries permit germline gene editing?

A logistics company can replace 800 warehouse workers with robots, reducing operating costs by 40% and ultimately lowering product prices for millions of consumers. Displaced workers would receive six months severance pay. Should the company proceed with full automation?

Workers have built livelihoods and identities around their jobs for decades, made life decisions based on stable employment, and fulfilled their contractual obligations. Replacing them with machines treats them purely as economic instruments. Should companies be permitted to automate away existing jobs at will?

Maria, 52, has worked in the same factory for 28 years. She has no college degree, supports two dependents, and lives in a region with few other employers. Her company plans to automate her entire department. Should companies be permitted to do this?

Economists estimate automation will displace 85 million jobs globally by 2030 but simultaneously create 97 million new roles requiring different skills. Historically, 67% of displaced industrial workers find re-employment within two years. Should companies be free to automate existing positions?

In most jurisdictions, employment law explicitly permits companies to restructure operations and eliminate positions for legitimate business reasons, with appropriate notice and severance. Given that automation-driven layoffs are fully legal, should companies pursue them?

A country can admit 100,000 refugees fleeing active conflict. Economic analyses project net fiscal costs of €2.3 billion over ten years, but also long-term integration producing €1.8 billion in GDP contributions and filling labor shortages. Should the country accept the refugees?

Refugees are human beings fleeing violence and persecution through no fault of their own. Turning them away means returning people to danger, violating basic human dignity and the principle of non-refoulement. Should countries accept refugees who present at their borders?

A family of five—two parents and three children aged 4, 7, and 12—have fled a war zone and arrived at a border crossing. They have no possessions, no country to return to, and nowhere else to go. Should the country admit this family?

Government data indicate refugees show a 12% higher unemployment rate than native citizens after five years of residence, but refugee children achieve educational outcomes equivalent to citizens within one generation. Given these integration statistics, should countries maintain open asylum policies?

The 1951 Refugee Convention, ratified by 145 nations, legally obliges signatory states to consider asylum claims and explicitly prohibits returning people to territories where they face persecution. Should countries fulfill this binding international legal obligation?

Research on capital punishment's deterrent effect is deeply contested: some studies claim 3–8 lives saved per execution through deterrence, others find no effect. Meanwhile, each execution costs an estimated $3 million more than life imprisonment. Should capital punishment be used as a criminal sanction?

Executing a citizen is an irreversible act that permanently denies them any possibility of rehabilitation, redemption, or exoneration if later found innocent. State killing violates the same principle as the murder it punishes. Should capital punishment exist as a legal sanction?

A man was executed for murder after a jury conviction. Twelve years later, DNA evidence conclusively proved he was innocent. His family received no compensation and no official apology. Given the irreversibility of execution, should capital punishment remain legal?

Since 1973, more than 190 death-row inmates in the United States have been exonerated. Statistical models estimate approximately 4% of all death sentences are imposed on factually innocent people. Given this documented error rate, should capital punishment be practised?

Capital punishment has been upheld as constitutional by supreme courts in multiple democratic nations and is affirmed through democratic legislative processes in those jurisdictions. Given its constitutional and legal status, should capital punishment be retained?

During a pandemic, vaccine misinformation spreading on social media was linked to 30% lower vaccination rates in heavily exposed regions, contributing to thousands of preventable deaths. Should platforms be required to proactively remove medically false content?

Freedom of speech is a fundamental right. Empowering governments or corporations to decide which claims qualify as 'misinformation' gives them authority to suppress legitimate dissent and inconvenient truths. Should platforms censor content that authorities label misinformation?

A mother refused to vaccinate her child after reading a viral misinformation post that falsely linked vaccines to autism. Her child died of a preventable disease. The post had remained live for 18 months before removal. Should platforms proactively remove medical misinformation before it spreads?

Content moderation systems have a documented 15% false-positive rate: approximately one in seven posts removed as misinformation is later found to be accurate. Given this error rate, should automated misinformation removal systems be deployed at scale?

The EU Digital Services Act legally obliges platforms with over 45 million EU users to assess and actively mitigate risks from misinformation. Platforms are bound by law to act against it. Should they comply and proactively remove misinformation content?

A government contact-tracing app that continuously tracks citizens' GPS locations reduced COVID-19 transmission by an estimated 25% in countries that mandated its use, preventing tens of thousands of deaths. Should governments deploy mandatory location-tracking health apps during epidemics?

Mandatory collection of citizens' location data by the government, even during a health crisis, violates the fundamental right to privacy and sets a precedent for mass surveillance that cannot easily be reversed once the emergency ends. Should it be permitted?

An abuse survivor's location data was collected by a mandatory government health app. A data contractor employee with access to the database—her abuser—used it to locate her new address. Should governments mandate location-tracking health apps?

Government health apps collect continuous location data from 98% of the population, but only 0.3% of the stored data is ever queried for contact tracing purposes. The remaining 99.7% is retained but never used. Should such comprehensive mandatory collection be justified?

Existing public health emergency statutes in many countries grant governments explicit legal authority to mandate disease surveillance measures, including location tracking of citizens. Given this legal authority, should governments deploy mandatory health tracking apps?
